text: I'd like to thank Sages, John, and Rick for the honor of the podium. I was asked to speak on laparoscopic protocolectomy and IPAA. As you've seen here, the commonest indication for that is ulcerative colitis, although about ten percent of the time it's also used for FAP.

When we're talking about operating for ulcerative colitis, basically our options are proctocolectomy and a brook ileostomy, or proctocolectomy with some kind of reconstruction done as either one stage, two stage, or in the very sick patients that Connor and John have mentioned as a three stage. I think as we've developed minimally invasive procedures, laparoscopy, and become more comfortable with the techniques, we've become less and less invasive. So we've gone from laparotomy, to laparoscopic assisted to more completely laparoscopic procedures. And that's what I'd like to talk about today.

So this evolution has been with a very complex operation and multiple incisions with the early attempts at lap pouches, simplified it with fewer incisions. And then when we try to get even more simple in terms of the number of incisions, that makes the procedure a little more complex to perform again.

So the more invasive procedures, obviously open laparotomy. I've also included in this the hybrid laparoscopic procedures where the surgeon will do the total colectomy laparoscopically and then make either a fan and steel or a small lower midline incision to do the rectal dissection. And some would disagree with me, but I think the hand-assisted procedures fall in this group too because the incision ends up being larger than you would actually use for extraction of the specimen. So here, just a illustration of laparotomy and the ileostomy. This would be a hybrid or hand assist with the laparoscopic incisions, and then either a fan and steel or a low midline for either the hand assist device or a hybrid procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon in a lateral to medial fashion, transforming it into a midline structure. After transecting the rectum, the entire colon and rectum are extracted through a small peri-umbilical incision. The procedure involves two laparoscopic ports and a third port in the right lower quadrant, which serves as the stoma site.

Just to compare hand assist and laparoscopic, Peter Marcello was the principal investigator on a randomized trial, which compared forty-seven hand assist and forty-eight laparoscopic and included both sigmoid colectomies and total colectomies. And you'll see in both the sigmoid colectomy and total colectomy, the operation was quicker in the hand assist group. There's a series of data from Mayo that compared a hundred laparoscopic cases with two hundred open. Within the lap group, seventy-five were lap assisted, twenty-five were hand assisted. Groups very similar in terms of outcomes except for the operative time which was longer in the laparoscopic group. Their post-op recovery was quicker in the laparoscopic group, length of stay was shorter.

Interestingly, in the laparoscopic group, the laparoscopic assisted cases took almost an hour less than the hand assist cases, which is the converse of Peter Marcello's findings. So how can we get even less invasive than that, than using a peri-umbilical extraction incision? Well, when the colon is fully mobilized and the mesentery is divided, then basically the colon can be exteriorized just about anywhere on the abdominal wall. So then it can be exteriorized through the ileostomy site, and we're not using a separate extraction site. And that's what I would refer to as completely laparoscopic.

Choosing patients for procedures, laparotomy, obviously any patient would be a candidate for that. Laparoscopic assisted would apply to most patients would be candidates for that. The completely laparoscopic and then moving to the single incision cases, typically patients at this point I think need to have a normal BMI. Extracting a colon in someone who's heavy through the ileostomy site requires the ileostomy site to be larger than it otherwise would be and can set the patient up potentially for problems with peristomal hernias, although that's not very likely as they would probably only have it for a couple of months.

Tips in terms of preparing for the procedures. Gravity is very helpful. We have steep position changes, so it's helpful to prepare the OR table carefully, position the patient carefully, with the sacrum over the table break to facilitate stapling or a mucosectomy. The thighs should be almost level with the abdomen so as not to interfere with the instruments when working at the flexures. So here's an example of the setup in the OA. Just a closer view showing the thighs are flat with the abdomen. The arms are wrapped and tucked. The hands are carefully protected with foam, have gel pads behind the elbows. And we always, always check the position of hands before replacing the foot plates at the end of the case.

A warm air blanket is placed over the patient's chest, and the chest is taped securely to the operating table. The surgical team performs a tilt test to ensure the patient remains stable on the table. The instrument tray is positioned at the foot of the table, and screens are set up on either side of the patient, allowing clear access for movement between the patient's ankle and shoulder during the procedure.

Basic steps can be broken up into five easy steps, a four-port technique. Do the left colon and splenic flexure first, then the right side, then dissect the rectum for the third step. The fourth step is exteriorizing and creation of the pouch, and then the last step is creating the anastomosis. Or you can make it difficult like that.

The surgical setup for a completely laparoscopic colectomy is shown, with a twelve millimeter port supraumbilical, a twelve millimeter port through the ileostomy site, and two five millimeter ports in a diamond configuration. The surgeon begins dissection at the left pelvic brim using a lateral to medial approach, maintaining the white line in the retroperitoneal plane with electrocautery. The left ureter and left peritoneal reflection are visible as the dissection progresses cephalad along the descending colon towards the spleen, which appears at the top of the screen.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal descending colon off the retroperitoneum, moving the plane medially closer to the colon. The distal transverse colon is then mobilized, with the option to enter the lesser sac from below, leaving the omentum with the patient, or from above, taking the omentum with the colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to manage the omentum, then turns to the right side after fully mobilizing the left side to the midline. Starting at the pelvic brim, the peritoneum is scored around the base of the cecum and terminal ileal mesentery. Using a lateral to medial approach, the ascending colon is mobilized from the retroperitoneum, with the white line remaining with the patient.

The surgeon uses a lateral to medial approach to preserve the ileocolic pedicle, applying careful traction and counter traction to clearly visualize the dissection plane. The right colon is fully mobilized up to the duodenum, visible at the bottom of the screen, where cold cutting is performed to avoid cautery near the duodenum. The hepatic flexure is taken after ensuring the duodenum is protected, and tissues are swept down towards the feet.

The surgeon identifies the correct retroperitoneal plane around the hepatic flexure, ensuring the colon remains vascularized during rectal dissection. The entire colon is mobilized before proceeding to the pelvic region.

The surgeon begins on the left side, entering the presacral space and extending the dissection medially and distally. The procedure then shifts to the right side, where the ureter is identified, and the rectum is elevated. The right parorectal peritoneum is scored, connecting with the previous left-side dissection. The team works further down in the pelvis, moving from lateral to anterior and towards the pelvic floor, revealing the shiny fascia over the pelvic floor muscles. In female patients, the uterus is suspended with a Keith needle, and a sponge stick is used in the vagina to retract and identify the rectovaginal septum. Before stapling, the extent of the dissection is checked digitally to ensure the staple line does not incorporate the back wall of the vagina.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to divide the mesentery, making incisions in the anterior and posterior fascia and separating muscle fibers. The port is removed from the right lower quadrant site, and the specimen is extracted through this incision, keeping the ileocolic pedicle intact.

So then we go ahead, transect, make the pouch. Tom, can you stop that video and go back to the PowerPoint, please? So here's an example. of a patient who had this done. The ileostomy, one of the five millimeter port sites becomes a drain site. We leave a drain in the pelvis. She's got that ten millimeter incision just above the umbilicus. The other two little holes there was because she wanted to keep her navel ring excess.

So can we make it even less invasive? And I'm just going to show a little bit of a clip here because I don't want to steal from Dan Geisler's talk in a few moments. So how about we just do the whole thing through the ileostomy site? There's a learning curve to this. We have to unlearn some of the lessons that we had to learn for laparoscopy. It's still possible to triangulate. It just is a little harder.

Use of articulated instruments makes things a little more difficult. We found a long laparoscope is helpful. It gets the scope out of the way and to either have a tall assistant to hold it or have them on a step also keeps them out of the way. Small movements are necessary because big movements move the camera. It's set up in the OR. It really requires cooperation of the whole team.

The surgeon completes the mobilization through a single incision, bringing the mesentery out through the ileostomy site. The ileocolic is divided close to the colon wall to preserve the ileocolic arcade, and the terminal ileum is transected to create a standard fifteen-centimeter J pouch. The pouch is returned to the abdomen, and a circular stapled anastomosis is created. The dissection is deep in the pelvis, with the camera positioned over the duodenum and retroperitoneum on the right side. The mesentery is straightened before firing the stapler, and an ileostomy is brought up.

We can stop the video and go back to the PowerPoint, please. So that was the specimen extracted through the ileostomy site and the final incision. So I'd like to thank you very much for your attention and allowing me to speak this afternoon. Thank you.
